title: Secure Edge: first-run network canvas & CLI-first install visual assets
preview:
Goal: Produce a cohesive set of onboarding visuals and product screenshots that support a CLI-first private deployment experience for Secure Edge (Redwood Private). This ticket covers: a network canvas storyboard for the operator flow, high-fidelity screenshots for the command-line installer and post-install Console snapshots, an upgrade/rollback visual pattern for air-gapped appliance upgrades, and developer-facing microcopy for each step.

Scope and deliverables: see `assets` list. Visual direction: minimal, operator-focused, emphasize connectivity state and safe-fallback affordances. Illustrate network topology as editable nodes (VPN, NAT, peering) rather than static diagrams so engineers can map their infra during first-run. Avoid marketing-style hero images; favor contextual screenshots + annotated overlays for docs.

Out-of-scope: marketing hero visuals, full localization deliverables (we will provide snapshot-ready text for i18n later), alternate color themes.

Links: Figma prototype (concept + screens): https://www.figma.com/file/secure-edge/onboard-cli-canvas (private); Confluence brief: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/OPS/Secure+Edge+first-run+brief; example GH PR with installer prompts: https://github.com/redwood-inference/private-installer/pull/271

Design constraints and notes: - Screens must be clear at 1200px content width for docs pages and 1440px for Console mockups. - Provide both dark and light background variants for screenshots used in docs. - Ensure terminal screenshots have selectable text overlays (PNG with separate caption file) so docs can copy commands easily. - Upgrade UI mockups must show version diff, preflight checks, estimated downtime, and rollback CTA. - Include a micro-interaction spec (120ms fade for connectivity state transitions, 200ms slide for node expand).

Acceptance criteria: - Delivered Figma file with named frames for each asset in `assets`. - 12 high-res PNG/JPG export images (1200–1440px wide) plus two animated GIFs: one short loop of network canvas interactions, one of the upgrade modal flow. - Copy approved by PM and Engineering (see comments). - Hand-off package in Drive with export pipeline and screenshot naming guide.

title: Miniature Request Lens: compact span timeline & correlation scroller
preview:
Goal: Design a compact, embeddable 'Miniature Request Lens' that fits into the Console request list and rollout detail panels for fast triage of problematic requests without navigating to the full trace inspector. The component should surface the most actionable information (critical spans, latency hotspots, correlation IDs, related traces) while preserving alignment with the Console's visual language and accessibility standards.

User problems we solve:
- Engineers and on-call SREs need a lightweight view to triage slow requests inline (list rows, rollout preview) without waiting for the heavy trace canvas to load.
- Product managers want a concise representation to compare pre/post-rollout performance in A/B rows.
- Designers need a consistent compact UI pattern to reuse across dashboards, alerts, and the rollout audit stream.

Design constraints and principles:
- Minimum footprint: target height 136px when collapsed in a table row and up to 320px when expanded in-place.
- Progressive disclosure: show a single horizontal micro-timeline with a density heatline, primary span chips (max 5), and a correlation ID affordance. Expand reveals timeline scrubber and a 'jump to full trace' action.
- Low cognitive load: color usage limited to status (ok/warn/err) and a neutral density palette for non-highlighted spans.
- Performance: SVG-based sparkline + request-level caching so the mini lens renders in < 120ms for typical list pages.
- Accessibility: keyboard focusable scrubber, ARIA labels for span chips, and contrast ratios per WCAG 2.1.

Proposed components (atomic breakdown):
- Micro-timeline sparkline (SVG) showing relative timing, shaded prefixes for cache hits and KV lookups.
- Span chips (compact pill) each with an icon for span type (http/db/kv), latency in ms, and hovercard with a 1-line operation name.
- Correlation scroller: a horizontal scroll area that shows related correlation IDs and their brief status (succeeded/errored/slow) so users can open sibling requests.
- Minimap scrubber: tappable scrub handle that updates a tooltip with absolute timestamps and span focus.
- Quick actions bar: hover-reveal actions for 'Open full trace', 'Pin to rollouts diff', and 'Create incident' (with prefilled fields).

Prototype notes and interactive flows:
- Prototype built in Figma (link below) with two main flows: (1) inline expand from request list, (2) view embedded inside rollout comparison row.
- Interaction: single-click expand, Ctrl+click opens full trace in new tab; scrub updates inline span highlight and triggers a small telemetry call to prefetch the trace payload.
- Mobile behavior: collapse to a single-line summary with the correlation badge and a chevron to full-screen mini-lens modal.

Design trade-offs discussed:
- Density vs. readability: we capped visible span chips at 5 and use a +N overflow indicator to avoid cramming. Some engineers asked for an option to switch to a 'dense mode' for debug sessions; deferred to follow-up.
- Inline caching: prefetching improves perceived speed but may lead to extra network calls; platform telemetry team requested a feature flag to gate aggressive prefetch.
- Visual fidelity vs. implementation cost: proposed SVG sparkline requires runtime sampling of span timings; ENG suggested a fallback static PNG for older browsers.

Feedback and decisions from reviews:
- 2026-02-25 (PM review - Lucia Ortega): "Love the correlation scroller for rollout comparisons. Ensure the quick actions map to the existing rollout audit events schema." -> Added dependency PM-903.
- 2026-02-28 (Engineering review - Rahul Patel): "Prefetch should be opt-in. We'll expose a prefetch config via infra and rely on KV cache headers to avoid extra work." -> Marked dependency ENG-48200 and added performance acceptance criterion.
- 2026-03-03 (Design critique - Nora Kim): "Chip labels need better truncation rules and accessible names. Use explicit ARIA labels with operation + latency." -> Implemented ARIA guidance.

Acceptance criteria (for handoff):
- Figma prototype with interactive scrub and hovercards available and linked; design token mapping completed.
- Edge-case states: empty spans, partial traces, and high-cardinality correlation lists rendered gracefully.
- Performance budget: first-paint of mini-lens <= 120ms on internal staging dataset.
- QA checklist: keyboard navigation, screen reader verification, and visual regression tests.

Implementation notes for engineers:
- Suggested data shape: request_summary { request_id, start_ts, end_ts, top_spans:[{op,type,duration_ms,status}], correlation_ids:[{id,status}], health_status } (note: this is guidance for API contract; final shape to be agreed with ENG).
- Prefetch flag name: tracing.mini_lens_prefetch (boolean).
- Telemetry events: mini_lens_open, mini_lens_scrub, mini_lens_action_click with minimal payload for privacy.

Artifacts and links:
- Figma prototype: https://www.figma.com/file/REDACTED/mini-lens-proto-2026
- Confluence spec: https://redwood.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/CONSOLE/pages/REDACTED/Miniature+Request+Lens+Spec
- Dev PR (placeholder): https://github.com/redwood-inference/console/pull/REDACTED
- Usability test notes: https://docs.google.com/document/d/REDACTED

Next steps:
- Resolve ENG-48200 on prefetch gating and finalize API contract (owner: Rahul Patel).
- Implement first-pass component in staging and run performance smoke test (owner: Marco Alvarez).
- Usability round with on-call engineers and PMs; iterate on chip truncation and action discoverability (owner: Ava Chen).

title: MiniCoach — inline micro-actions for cost literacy during rollout setup
preview: Problem: users configuring model rollouts and capacity commits often lack quick, contextual guidance about the immediate cost impact of toggles (batching, KV cache, quantization). Large doc-driven onboarding interrupts flow. Goal: design a compact, dismissible "MiniCoach" micro-overlay that surfaces a single actionable tip tied to the control the user is interacting with, with a one-tap action (apply suggestion) and a link to deeper docs. The component should be small, non-modal, accessible, localizable, and trackable for analytics.

title: Glassweight token harmonization for mixed-brand consoles and high-density table visuals in low-light mode
preview:
Background: Multiple brand integrations and a recent push toward translucent "glass" surfaces have exposed inconsistencies in dark-mode token mappings. High-density table rows, compact controls, and chart stroke/area visibility suffer when brand accent intensity collides with contrast guardrails. This ticket harmonizes a small, pragmatic token set ("glassweight" family) and defines component-level overrides so partners can opt into brand intensity without breaking accessibility or telemetry dashboards.

Goals:
- Define a concise glassweight token family for dark themes that controls surface translucency, tint strength, blur/opacity trade-offs, and micro-elevation for compact components.
- Map existing brand-accent tokens to conservative dark-mode fallbacks with quantized strength levels (low/medium/high) for predictable behavior.
- Introduce per-component override rules for tables, charts, and compact buttons so visual density and legibility are preserved.
- Provide a migration guide and automated codemod to update console components to the new tokens.

Non-goals:
- Do not change core brand color palettes themselves. This work is about mapping and overrides for the console surface in dark mode only.
- Not addressing mobile platform-specific rendering optimizations (separate ticket).

Success metrics:
- No critical contrast regressions in our eval suite (contrast ratio >= 3.0 for chart lines and >= 4.5 for primary button text in dense table rows).
- A/B test on 5 internal dashboards shows <= 0.5% increase in page render time attributable to visual style changes (target: negligible perf regression).
- Rollout plan accepted and togglable per-tenant in Dedicated/Private deployments.

title: Compact template-app onboarding: reference screenshots & README flow diagrams
preview:
Goal: Produce a compact set of reference screenshots and a minimal README flow diagram for the Template Apps quickstart that fit within the new docs compact layout.

Context:
- The docs team is introducing a condensed quickstart card for template apps used on the /quickstarts page. We need 6–8 annotated screenshots (desktop + one mobile highlight) and a single-page README flow diagram that explains first-run behavior and environment variables.
- Target audience: developers trying Redwood Inference with a template app (Node + Next.js starter). Focus on clarity, minimal copy, and visual parity with existing console UI (rollouts, model selector, logs).

Deliverables (acceptance criteria):
1) 6 annotated reference screenshots exported at 1440px wide (PNG) and 640px wide (mobile crop) that include: 1) repo clone + .env checklist, 2) local dev server running with Redwood token input modal, 3) console: model selector and latency-cost badge, 4) first call response showing streaming output, 5) rollout toggle + canary sample, 6) telemetry panel with token cost breakdown. Screens should be consistent with Figma component library and use Redwood colors/typography.
2) Single-page README flow diagram (SVG + PNG) that maps: Clone -> Install -> Set ENV -> Start -> Console Connect -> Make First Call -> View Telemetry. Diagram must be export-friendly for docs (800px height).
3) A README snippet (md) with inline images and alt text, copy limited to 120 characters per step.
4) A small captioned gallery for the quickstart card (3 images: hero, streaming, telemetry) sized for fast CDN delivery.

Notes on approach/constraints:
- Keep overlays minimal: use semi-transparent labels for key UI elements rather than full UI mockups. Avoid exposing any real API keys in images.
- Use generic sample content in responses (no PHI).
- Mobile crops should center on the chat stream and model selector for legibility.
- Follow accessibility contrast guidelines for annotation text (AA).

Progress updates / decisions / feedback log:
- 2026-02-12 (Aisha): Draft Figma file created: https://www.figma.com/file/REDWOOD-TEMPLATE-ONBOARDING (internal). Started with 8 screenshot frames.
- 2026-02-18 (Mateo): Reduced scope to 6 reference screens + 3-card gallery to speed up handoff for docs. Added acceptance criteria for image sizes.
- 2026-02-22 (PM - Lina Chen): Confirmed content scope with PM. Added explicit step for telemetry panel. PM requested copy that emphasizes cost transparency.
- 2026-02-26 (Engineering - Javier Ortiz): Flagged that streaming response state may differ locally; recommended using recorded sample JSON and static snapshot to avoid flakiness. Added link to sample response: https://github.com/redwood-inference/examples/blob/main/template-apps/sample_responses/streaming.json
- 2026-03-03 (Design review): Design review notes from Olivia Park: reduce annotation density, increase label font to 12px, and ensure rollouts toggle state is visible at 2x. Action: implement updates and export new assets.
- 2026-03-06 (Dev review): ENG-9072 provided a lightweight mock endpoint that returns deterministic output for screenshots. Confirmed by Mateo.
- 2026-03-09 (Copy review - Samir Patel): Shortened step copy to meet 120-char limit and added alt-text suggestions for each image.
- 2026-03-10 (This ticket): Assets uploaded to Drive and PR opened for docs insertion. Waiting on visual QA from docs team and final signoff from PM.

Links & artifacts:
- Figma master (internal): https://www.figma.com/file/REDWOOD-TEMPLATE-ONBOARDING
- Drive: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/REDWOOD_TEMPLATE_ONBOARDING_ASSETS
- Sample response (github): https://github.com/redwood-inference/examples/tree/main/template-apps/sample_responses
- Docs PR (draft): https://github.com/redwood-inference/docs/pull/542

Open items / blockers:
- Visual QA by docs (requested 2026-03-10) for image export compression settings (PNG vs WebP).
- Confirm final hero image crop used by marketing for CDN (need marketing input by 2026-03-13).

Acceptance checklist (to be checked off in PR):
- [ ] All 6 desktop PNGs exported and verified at 1440px width
- [ ] Mobile crops exported at 640px width and visually inspected
- [ ] README flow diagram exported as SVG + PNG and added to PR
- [ ] README snippet (md) with inline images and alt text included in PR
- [ ] Gallery captions written and approved by PM
- [ ] Docs PR updated and assigned to docs reviewer

If any of the blockers remain past 2026-03-13, escalate to Lina Chen (PM) for prioritization.

title: Improve table interaction parity and runtime performance
preview:
Problem: Our table component has diverging behavior across density modes and struggles with large datasets. Users reported janky column-resize interactions, inconsistent keyboard focus when using virtualized rows, and confusing filter/sort affordances when density is compact. This ticket scopes a design and token update to deliver interaction parity across density modes, cleaner resize affordances, and layouts that enable engineering to implement efficient virtualization (variable row heights + sticky headers).

Goals:
- Establish consistent resize handles & hit targets across compact/comfortable/expanded density tokens.
- Define keyboard and screen-reader flow for column resize, reordering, and sorting that works with virtualization.
- Create visual and microinteraction specs for sorting and multi-column filters that scale to narrow column widths.
- Provide updated spacing/color/icon tokens to reduce layout thrashing and enable engineering batching/caching optimizations.

Scope excludes: server-side filtering UX, data modeling, and backend pagination behavior (handled by ENG-2101).

This ticket covers high-level UX flows, chart-level specs for three canonical correlation views, prototype links, and notes for handoff to frontend and infra for data requirements.

title: Introduce expressive-outline variant and compact icon-stack button family
preview: Motivation: recent product requests and QA reports show inconsistent outline contrast across dark/high-contrast themes, and several surface-level regressions where loading spinners overlay icons incorrectly on compact icon buttons. This ticket introduces a new 'expressive-outline' variant (higher stroke contrast + slightly heavier on hover) and a compact 'icon-stack' family that allows stacked primary/icon combinations for dense rows and mobile lists. Work includes token mapping, visual specs for light/dark/high-contrast, updated loading rules (spinner -> inline skeleton or replace icon), Storybook stories, and accessibility verification.

title: Pulse Inspector: GPU pool health timeline, autoscale tuning & burst policy editor
preview:
Goal: Create a compact, operator-focused inspector panel (codename: Pulse) for Dedicated GPU pools that merges health timeline, topology status, autoscaling controls, and a burst policy editor into a single slide-over UI. The inspector must be glanceable for on-call engineers and also provide safe editing flows for SREs and platform owners.

Scope: initial design covers single-pool view (no multi-select), timeline-based health overview, recent incidents, autoscale target tuning (min/target/max + responsive knobs), burst budget editor, cost impact preview, simple rollback/canary controls, and contextual links to detailed pool logs and metrics dashboards.

Out of scope: full cluster topology management, user-level access controls (handled elsewhere), and deep cost forecasting (only an immediate per-hour estimate shown).

title: Align typography, spacing, and color ramps for charts and tables
preview:
Purpose: Reduce visual drift across data-heavy components (tables, charts, cards) by consolidating color ramps, tightening the typography scale at small sizes, and standardizing spacing and corner radii. Motivation: recent QA and customer feedback called out inconsistent row padding, low-contrast legends in charts, and varied shadow strength between card components. This ticket scopes token changes (color, type, spacing, radius, elevation), the compatibility strategy, and delivery plan for the March DS release.

Proposed changes (high level):
- Color: introduce a unified neutral ramp for data surfaces (neutral-050..neutral-900) and a separate semantic ramp for data accents (accent-050..accent-700). Increase contrast for legend/text-on-data to meet AA for 12px minimum sizes. Update primary/secondary swatches used in charts to reduce vibrancy clashes.
- Typography: tighten small-size scale: set body-xs 12/16, caption 11/14, table-cell 12/16; reduce line-height variance to improve row alignment. Document token names (type-xs, type-sm, type-base).
- Spacing/radius: standardize table row vertical padding to spacing-2 (8px) and spacing-3 (12px) as toggles; unify card radius to radius-2 (8px) for medium surfaces, radius-1 (4px) for compact controls.
- Shadow/elevation: reduce shadow spread for floating data cards to a subtle elevation (e-1), reserve e-2 for modal stacks; provide a compact shadow token set optimized for 1px device scale to avoid overly soft shadows on small cards.

Acceptance criteria:
- Tokens published to the design tokens repo with clear migration docs and code examples for React components.
- Charts and table components updated in the component library storybook demonstrating before/after visuals.
- Legend and table text meet WCAG AA for minimum 12px sizes in both dark and light themes.
- Frontend integration PRs created with feature flags for a canary rollout.

Rollout plan:
1) Publish token file and mapping guide.
2) Swap tokens in isolated chart + table stories and run visual diffs.
3) Create frontend PR to toggle tokens behind a runtime flag for 10% of users (canary).
4) Collect metrics (error/visual regressions/feedback) for 2 weeks, iterate.
5) Full rollout in design-system-1.9 if no regressions.

Notes on migration: Avoid renaming core tokens used widely (like spacing-1..4). Instead introduce alias tokens (eg. data-row-padding -> spacing-2) and deprecate old names with a 2-release window. Work with ENG to add a compatibility layer in token loader so older releases can map automatically.

Links and artifacts:
- Figma board: https://www.figma.com/file/REDACTED/data-tokens
- Storybook mocks (internal): https://storybook.redwood.ai/ds/data-tokens
- Draft PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/design-tokens/pull/124

Risks and mitigations:
- Risk: Small text in dense tables may still fail contrast if customers use custom theme overrides. Mitigation: add linting checks to CI and doc guidance for theme authors.
- Risk: Shadow changes may look flatter on high-DPI devices. Mitigation: include device-specific token suggestions and test on macOS Retina and common Android devices.

title: normalize-variant-axes-and-auto-layout-prune-for-data-cards
preview: Objective: Clean up the Data Card component family in the Figma library to reduce variant combinatorial bloat, enforce predictable auto-layout behavior inside dense table/cell contexts, and align naming to the semantic token taxonomy. Scope: - Audit all Data Card instances used in Tables, Charts, and dense list views. - Consolidate variant axes (size / density / state) into a minimal contract that supports: compact, regular, interactive, disabled. - Remove deprecated nested auto-layout frames that cause collapse/flicker when constraints change. - Update token references (spacing, type, color) to use semantic tokens instead of hard-coded values. Out of scope: visual redesigns or feature additions to Data Card content. This ticket focuses on library hygiene and publish readiness.

title: Traffic Inspector & Explainable Rollback microflow — prototype and review
preview: Design a compact, developer-friendly Traffic Inspector panel and an ‘Explainable Rollback’ microflow for the Redwood Console rollouts experience. The goal is to give engineers and PMs a way to: 1) quickly inspect live traffic splits and recent decisioning (why traffic moved to variant B), 2) view the event timeline (errors, latency spikes, eval regressions) correlated with traffic changes, and 3) initiate a rollback with an explanation assistant that recommends rollback scope (full, partial, or target segment) and required follow-ups. This ticket covers low-fidelity flows, an interactive Figma prototype for usability testing with two SREs, and a short design review with Eng to align on telemetry landmarks we must surface in the UI.

title: Resilient mini-spark canvas: semantic prioritization, selective-announcer, and GPU-fallback strategy
preview: Replace current mini-sparkline rendering for dense time-series with a resilient canvas-first implementation that prioritizes semantic content for screen readers and implements a compact tooltip eviction policy to reduce CPU main-thread pressure. Deliver a pattern that gracefully falls back to CPU-lit SVG rendering when GPU/OffscreenCanvas is not available or when memory pressure crosses a deterministic threshold.

Deliver a pattern that gracefully falls back to CPU-lit SVG rendering when GPU/OffscreenCanvas is not available or when memory pressure crosses a deterministic threshold.

title: Horizon-band virtualization for long-running series & an a11y-first legend summarizer
preview: Problem: Time-series charts used in telemetry dashboards commonly display days-to-months of data. Rendering full-resolution lines for very long series kills interactivity and memory, and the legend becomes unusable for dozens of series. We need a design that maintains readable semantics for screen readers, preserves developer-configurable color tokens, and yields a progressive, performant render path across devices.

title: Retargeting creative kit (Lifecycle Growth Sprint wk3): dev-first variants + exports
preview:
## Context
We’re launching Week 3 retargeting as part of the **Lifecycle Growth Experiments Sprint** (Hosted API). This ticket produces a small, reusable **retargeting creative kit** for warm audiences (site visitors / signup-no-activation / activated-no-return).

Primary goal: downstream activation lift (First successful request within 7 days), not CTR-only.

## Audience stages (creative should map to state)
- **A: Site visitor (no signup):** “Come back and try it” → quickstart/product page.
- **B: Signup, no activation:** “Finish setup / run minimal request” → console login / quickstart.
- **C: Activated, no return:** “Next value step” → usage dashboard / streaming docs.

## Messaging guardrails (must follow)
- Dev-first, concrete, minimal hype.
- Avoid unverified quantitative claims (latency/cost/uplift). Use directional language ("built for", "designed to").
- Avoid personalization language like “We noticed you…”.
- No enterprise-heavy positioning as the primary headline for self-serve cohorts.
- Ensure CTAs match landing pages exactly ("Get started", "Finish setup", "Open Console", etc.).

Reference: onboarding messaging guidelines + retargeting creatives brief.

## Deliverables
### Concepts (minimum)
Deliver **3 concepts**, each with **2 copy variants** (v1/v2), sized for core placements.

Concept 1 — **Activation flow (Audience B)**
- Visual: simple 3-step flow diagram: Signup → API key → First request
- Copy direction: “Make your first successful request in minutes” / “Finish setup: create an API key”
- CTA: Finish setup / Make first request

Concept 2 — **Cost + latency visibility (Audience C)**
- Visual: product/console dashboard-style frame (illustrative; avoid unreadable micro-text)
- Copy direction: “See token usage, latency, and errors per route”
- CTA: Open Console / View dashboard

Concept 3 — **Dev snippet / Hosted API (Audience A)**
- Visual: text-forward creative with a readable curl or TypeScript snippet
- Copy direction: “Hosted LLM inference you can ship” / “Copy/paste a minimal request”
- CTA: Get started / View quickstart

Optional (only if on time): Concept 4 — **Streaming** variant (Audience B/C)
- Visual: terminal-style “streaming output” lines, high contrast
- Copy direction: “See tokens sooner with streaming”
- CTA: Try streaming

### Sizes
- 1200×628 (landscape)
- 1080×1080 (square)
- 1080×1920 (story) — only for concepts that remain readable in vertical crops

### Export + handoff requirements
- Final exports: PNG (preferred) or JPG, < 1.5MB when possible.
- Provide a consistent naming scheme that matches UTM `utm_content` keys.
- Provide source file link (Figma) with components organized by concept/variant/size.

## File naming (must match attribution/UTM)
Format: `{concept}_{variant}_{size}`
- concept: activationflow | costlatency | hostedapi | streaming
- variant: v1 | v2
- size: 1200x628 | 1080x1080 | 1080x1920

Examples:
- activationflow_v1_1200x628.png
- hostedapi_v2_1080x1080.png

## Acceptance criteria
- At least **6 static creatives** total (3 concepts × 2 variants) delivered in **1200×628 + 1080×1080**.
- At least **one “pure dev” text-forward** variant (snippet-heavy, minimal decoration).
- Text legible on mobile (no tiny subcopy/disclaimers).
- Redwood logo present; product labeled “Redwood API” or “Hosted API”.
- No stock robot/brain visuals.
- Messaging review sign-off (Soojin) + Ops QA readiness (Tyler) before upload.

## Review plan
1) Internal design review (Tessa) — layout + readability.
2) Messaging review (Soojin) — claims + tone + CTA alignment.
3) Growth/Ops QA (Ben/Tyler) — naming/UTMs + platform constraints.

## Open questions (resolve during execution)
- Whether to include any “Hosted → Dedicated/Private later” line: default is **NO** unless there’s whitespace and it doesn’t steal focus from activation.
- Confirm landing URL per audience (needed for CTA exact wording); if not finalized, keep CTA generic (“Get started” / “Open Console”).

title: Refresh onboarding email template (v2): layout, typography, and mobile responsiveness
preview:
Update the HTML email template used for the Hosted API onboarding drip v2 to improve readability, copy fit, and mobile responsiveness across common clients (Gmail, iOS Mail, Outlook). Ensure the template supports A/B subject + headline tests, dynamic sections (by segment), and compliance/footer requirements.

Context
- This template will be used across Week 1 email A/B and subsequent iterations.
- Must align with messaging guidelines: performance + reliability, avoid over-claiming, keep copy developer-first.
- Must not harm deliverability (no heavy imagery; keep text-forward).

Goals / success criteria
- Mobile: no horizontal scroll at 320px; comfortable line length; CTA visible above the fold on iPhone 13/SE.
- Copy fit: supports long subject lines and longer code snippets without breaking layout.
- Accessibility: color contrast, readable typography, link/CTA affordance, meaningful preheader.
- Operational: easy for Marketing Ops to swap modules (hero, bullets, code block, CTA, footer) without hand-editing fragile tables.

Non-goals
- Major brand refresh or new illustration system.
- Rewriting the sequence copy (owned in PM-659 / copy doc); we’ll provide fit feedback + suggested trims.

Deliverables
1) Figma spec for the v2 email template: desktop + mobile layout, typography scale, spacing, component states.
2) Final HTML module guidance (what’s safe across clients; do/don’t list).
3) Copy-fit recommendations: max character counts for subject, headline, subhead, CTA.
4) QA screenshots across major clients (Litmus pass list).

Links
- Confluence design spec (email v2): sources/confluence/product-docs/design-specs/email-onboarding-drip-v2.json
- Messaging guidelines: sources/confluence/sales-enablement/positioning-and-messaging/onboarding-messaging-guidelines-hosted-api.json
- Draft copy variants: sources/google_drive/shared_drives/go-to-market/onboarding-email-copy-variants-v2.json
- Send schedule/triggers: sources/google_drive/shared_drives/go-to-market/onboarding-email-send-schedule-and-triggers.json

title: Compact aggregate announcer + render-decimation for high-density time-series
preview:
Problem:
High-density time-series views (dashboards with many small series or very long histories) are causing two user-facing issues: 1) screen-reader consumers get overwhelmed by thousands of live announcements when the chart updates, and 2) interactive latency spikes (hover/tooltip + legend updates) as DOM nodes and per-series layers multiply.

Goal:
Design a lightweight, accessible pattern and visual affordance that: 1) collapses non-essential series into an ARIA-friendly summary that screen readers can consume without noise, 2) preserves discoverability and keyboard navigation for power users, and 3) reduces DOM and paint overhead to improve interactive latency for common dashboard workloads.

Constraints:
- Must be implementable without breaking existing SDK consumers of chart components.
- Must respect tokenized color and spacing system (so theme changes cascade).
- Should provide clear fallback for low-density mode (no change to current experience).
- Must meet Redwood console accessibility baseline and SLOs for interaction latency.

Proposed solution (high level):
1) Compact Aggregate Announcer: an opt-in chart mode that groups low-importance series into a single "aggregate" cluster. For assistive tech we expose a single ARIA region summarizing the hidden series (count, min/max/last value, top-changed series) and an explicit "expand" control that keyboard + SR users can trigger to enumerate items on demand.
2) Render-decimation pipeline: introduce a rendering pre-pass that computes a tile-level density map and decides per-tile rendering strategy: full vector for focused tiles, decimated canvas raster for background tiles, and a static image snapshot for extremely dense/idle tiles. This reduces per-series repaint and DOM churn.
3) Legend & tooltip strategy: replace per-series legend DOM nodes with a virtualized legend list. Tooltips for hidden/aggregated series are surfaced in the aggregate summary panel rather than as individual popovers.
4) Token alignment: add two new design tokens (chart.aggregation-badge-color, chart.aggregate-announcer-font-size) and update the compact legend token set to ensure visual parity between expanded and collapsed states.

Trade-offs considered:
- Hiding series by default reduces discoverability for novice users. Mitigation: show visually prominent soft-hint ("N collapsed") with one-click expand, and keep expand control in keyboard tab order.
- Rasterization sacrifices crispness for performance on extreme zooms. Mitigation: prefer vector for focused tiles and only rasterize background tiles; provide device-pixel-ratio-aware snapshots.

Success metrics / acceptance criteria:
- Screen reader tests: SR walkthrough of a 200+ series chart yields a single concise summary read by NVDA/VoiceOver by default, with an expand action that reads per-series items on demand.
- Performance: for a synthetic dashboard with 5K series (resampled to display resolution), interactive hover latency (time between mousemove and visible hover highlight update) improves by >= 40% compared to baseline.
- DOM nodes: per-chart DOM nodes reduced by >= 70% for high-density views.
- Visual parity: default view for under-20-series charts is identical to current UX.

Deliverables:
- Design mockups (compact + expanded states) and microinteraction spec (enter/exit aggregate, keyboard flows).
- New token suggestions and token migration notes for design system owner.
- Prototype: interactive Figma with overlay annotations + small React prototype integrating virtualized legend list.
- Accessibility test plan and scripts for manual SR testing.

Acceptance checklist:
- [ ] Mockups reviewed and approved by PM (Lina Gomez) and Engineering lead (Mateo Ruiz).
- [ ] Token PR submitted to design-tokens repo with fallback tokens.
- [ ] Prototype PR with smoke perf measurements attached.
- [ ] Usability session with 4 participants including 1 screen-reader user scheduled and completed.

Notes on related work:
- This ticket intersects with ENG-21012 (rendering pipeline refactor) and PM-502 (dashboard aggregation UX). Coordinate pre-deploy contract with runtime team to avoid double work.

title: Token Spend Explorer — Role-aware Attribution & Pricing Presets
preview:
Goal: Design a Token Spend Explorer UX that gives billing owners and engineers a single-pane view to understand token-level spend across models, routes, and users, and to export cost-attribution results. The view must support role-aware defaults (Billing Admin vs Engineering Lead), pricing presets (flat-rate, slabed-tiers, custom per-model overrides), and an export wizard that produces a reconciliable CSV for accounting.

Scope:
- Main explorer surface: filter bar (time range, model, route, project tag, user role), column composer (tokens, prompt tokens, completion tokens, cost, cost-per-1k, cache-savings estimate), and grouped breakdown cards (by route, by model, by tag).
- Attribution modes: direct token attribution (by request), proportional attribution (split across route components), and cohort-level allocation (by project or customer tag).
- Pricing presets: built-in templates (Hosted-default, Dedicated-commit, Enterprise-custom) plus UI to create & apply a custom pricing profile that affects computed cost previews.
- Export wizard: stepper that selects attribution mode, pricing profile, columns, and reconciliation keys (request_id, session_id). Output: CSV and downloadable JSON lines for audit ingestion.
- Role-aware UI: Billing Admin sees pricing presets and export columns by default; Engineer sees latency/cost tradeoffs and sample prompts usage in addition to cost.

Design constraints and non-goals:
- Does not implement live cost syncing to billing system; export must be reconciliable but ingestion is a separate integration task.
- Does not include per-token content inspection; only metadata-driven attribution.

UX goals:
- Fast insight: median cost-per-call and 95th percentile token spikes at top of page.
- Explainability: tooltip-friendly equations showing how cost derived from tokens + pricing profile.
- Confidence for finance: export includes checksums and a provenance header with model version, pricing profile id, and time window.

Acceptance criteria:
- Prototype demonstrates switching attribution modes and shows immediate cost recalculation.
- Export wizard produces a sample CSV (<= 100 rows) for reviewer validation.
- Role-aware defaults applied and visible in header.
- Accessibility: all interactive elements keyboard-focusable and color contrast meets AA.

Design artifacts and links: [Figma token spend explorer prototype] https://www.figma.com/file/XXXX/Token-Spend-Explorer, [Design brief] https://docs.redwood.ai/confluence/token-cost-explorer, [Sample export spec] https://drive.redwood.ai/exports/token_export_spec_v1.csv

title: Adaptive Pool Tuner: Predictive Cost & Latency Tradeoff Canvas
preview:
Problem: Operators of dedicated GPU pools need a fast way to tune autoscale and burst settings while understanding the expected cost and latency tradeoffs for the observed workload. Current pool controls are distributed across multiple panels; users must toggle between health, scaling policy, and cost dashboards to make a single configuration change. This leads to conservative settings, overprovisioning, and surprise bills.

Goal: Design a single, compact canvas — the Adaptive Pool Tuner — that surfaces predicted tail latency, median latency, and incremental cost for proposed autoscale and burst rule changes. The canvas should support short iterative workflows: propose change, simulate effect on a chosen workload profile, preview cost delta and SLO impact, save as draft or apply with rollout policy.

Scope:
- A compact left rail with workload selector (recent routes, custom trace upload).
- Central visualization showing current vs. simulated latency percentiles and throughput at configurable time slices.
- Right rail cost summary with per-hour and per-24h delta, and suggestions (e.g., reduce max-burst, increase cold-scale delay).
- Inline canary rollout controls: percent, duration, automatic rollback threshold.
- Export links to runbook and annotated snapshot for on-call.

Out of scope for this ticket: backend simulation engine; we will use a mocked predictive API for prototype. Deeper cost-model accuracy and multi-pool orchestration will be follow-ups.

Success metrics:
- Reduce number of console pages visited per tuning session to 1 (tracked via analytics).
- 30% fewer manual scaling overrides per pool in the first 90 days after rollout (pilot customers).
- Time-to-decision for ops engineers < 3 minutes for common tuning tasks.

Design constraints & considerations:
- Must be usable on 1280px width with collapsed left nav (compact mode).
- Accessibility: color contrast for visual bands, keyboard navigable controls, aria labels for simulation chart nodes.
- Mobile: view-only mode for on-call responders (not a full edit flow).
- Trust: show confidence band in simulations and a short note when predictions rely on sampled traces.

Deliverables:
- Low-fidelity flows (edge cases: high-variance workloads, sudden spike simulation).
- High-fidelity Figma prototype with interactive simulation slider and rollout config.
- Design spec (tokens, spacing, microcopy) and annotated accessibility checklist.
- Usability test plan for 6 internal operators + 2 pilot customers.

Links to artifacts: Figma: https://www.figma.com/file/EXAMPLE/adaptive-pool-tuner (prototype v1) | Confluence spec: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/INF/Adaptive+Pool+Tuner | Mock API for sim: https://github.com/redwood-inference/pool-sim-mock/pull/14

title: Signal Flow Navigator — lineage and lightweight request exploration
preview:
Problem: Engineers need a fast, low-friction way to explore request lineage across services without the cognitive overhead of a full-forensics trace workspace. Current consoles focus on dense timelines and span-level forensic tools; customers asked for a lightweight explorer that surfaces end-to-end causality, correlation IDs, and cost/latency overlays for quick debugging and rollout validation.

Goal: Design a compact, keyboard-first 'Signal Flow Navigator' that lets users: 1) search by correlation ID or route, 2) see a condensed lineage graph + linear time-strip, 3) toggle span-grouping and sampling, and 4) overlay per-request cost and latency heatmarks. It must integrate with rollouts so PM/SEs can validate canaries and quickly escalate or rollback.

Scope: UX surface + clickable prototype (Figma) for console team review; include acceptance criteria, accessibility considerations, telemetry events to track, and handoff notes for frontend engineering. Not in scope: backend sampling pipeline changes (will be tracked as ENG deps).

title: Token Waterfall Slate: compact attribution + export patterns
preview: Design and prototype a compact, high-density "Waterfall Slate" for the Redwood Console cost dashboard that surfaces token-level attribution (per-route, per-model, per-invocation) and supports multi-format export. The slate should present a layered token-cost waterfall (origin layers: prompt, completion, KV cache hit, prefix reuse, retries), allow quick pivoting by dimension (route, workspace, model family, quantization profile), and provide guided pricing-simulation controls (unit rate overrides, slab presets, reserved capacity adjustments). The goal is a single-pane summary that is readable for both engineers and finance stakeholders, with progressive disclosure for forensic detail and an export format suited for downstream chargeback and BI ingestion.

title: Dark-mode interaction ink reflow and graph legend hierarchy
preview: Problem: In deep-dark console themes interactive elements (buttons, menus, toggles) exhibit visual artifacts and perceived weight due to 'ink' reflow and overlay rules. Chart legends and color keys lose hierarchy against near-black surfaces, and dense tables depend on thin separators that drop below readable contrast at small sizes. Scope: Create a token mapping and component override proposal for deep/dense dark surfaces that preserves accessible contrast, preserves brand accent identity, and avoids visual clutter across tables, charts, and controls. Deliverables: 1) Token transform rules (alpha overlays, elevation-strength multipliers, accent-saturation clamp) 2) Component override spec for Button / Menu / Toggle / Table row / Chart legend 3) Figma tokens and component variants 4) Implementation guidance for theme engine (runtime mapping + opt-in canary) 5) Automated contrast tests and visual regression sets.

title: Incident-aware Upgrade Schedule Editor for Private Clusters
preview:
Problem: Operators of private/VPC clusters need a single, actionable place in the Console to schedule cluster upgrades while taking active incidents, maintenance windows, and customer SLOs into account. Current upgrade scheduling is too static: it doesn't show ongoing incidents, doesn't suggest safe canary windows, and lacks a clear rollback/abort affordance for mid-upgrade incidents.

Goal: Design an interactive Upgrade Schedule Editor that: 1) surfaces current incident state and cluster health when picking times; 2) suggests safe windows based on SLA/SLO and past busy-hours telemetry; 3) supports canary waves, staged rollouts, and quick abort/rollback actions; 4) integrates RBAC so only authorized personas can change critical windows; and 5) provides clear audit trail + links to incident runbooks.

Scope of this ticket: UX prototypes and validation for the Editor (desktop & compact tablet widths), microcopy for alerts/warnings, edge-case flows (ongoing incident, multiple clusters in a pool, cross-region dependencies), and final handoff assets for engineering. Not in scope: backend scheduling engine work, feature flagging logic, or on-prem installer changes (those are separate ENG tickets).

Key user stories:
- As an SRE, I want the editor to warn me if an active incident affects >10% of nodes so I can delay the upgrade.
- As a Platform PM, I want suggested canary windows that minimize customer impact based on historical peak hours.
- As an Operator, I want a single-click abort that stops rollout and triggers rollback playbooks.
- As an Admin, I want audit metadata (who scheduled, what changed) surfaced in the timeline and exportable as CSV.

title: Privileged role grant review & audit UX for Console
preview:
Problem: Admins need a clear, low-friction way to grant, review, and revoke privileged roles (e.g., system-admin, billing-admin, oncall-privileged) while ensuring every change is auditable and can be justified for compliance. Current console path buries audit metadata and the member assignment flow creates accidental over-grants because scopes and resource boundaries are not surfaced.

Goal: Design a compact role grant review surface in Console that: 1) surfaces resource scoping and active sessions for the member being assigned; 2) flags high-risk grants with inline justifications and mandatory commit notes for P0/P1 roles; 3) integrates with audit log links and one-click revoke/impersonate for validation; 4) preserves quick bulk workflows for SRE/platform teams while preventing accidental permanent elevation.

Scope: UI/UX for role editor and member assignment flows only (Console). Non-goals: backend policy engine changes, SSO mapping logic, or RBAC enforcement semantics (these are separate eng tickets).

Key UX principles: minimal friction for routine ops, mandatory friction for high-risk actions, continuous discoverability of audit context, and clear affordances for temporary grants and review windows.

title: Batching & Cache Setup Wizard — onboarding flow and safety guardrails
preview: Design an end-to-end onboarding wizard inside Redwood Console that helps engineers and platform owners configure batching and KV/prefix caching for routes and model profiles. The wizard should: 1) quickly recommend safe defaults based on measured workload signals (median seq len, token rate, request size distribution), 2) explain trade-offs (latency vs cost vs variance) with short contextual explainers, 3) provide a simulated cost & latency preview for chosen presets and slider settings, 4) expose advanced controls in a scoped ‘expert’ panel with JSON preview and validation, and 5) enforce guardrails and warnings for settings that would violate SLOs or exceed estimated spend. This ticket covers the visual flows, microcopy, onboarding checklist, one progressive disclosure prototype in Figma, and a short pilot usability test with 6 backend engineers and 4 ML infra users.
